EU Automotive Policy Changes What They Mean for the UK Car Market

**EU Automotive Policy Changes: What It Means for Manufacturers, Drivers and the UK Market**

The European automotive industry has entered a new phase of transition. Over the past few days, the EU has agreed on new rules for the automotive sector as part of its circular economy strategy, while at the same time signalling a more flexible approach to the long-planned ban on new petrol and diesel vehicles.

For manufacturers, fleets and drivers alike, this marks an important moment — one that balances environmental ambition with commercial reality.

**A Shift Towards Circular Economy Thinking**

The EU’s latest agreement focuses on how vehicles are designed, built and managed throughout their entire lifecycle. The emphasis is no longer just on tailpipe emissions, but on sustainability from production to disposal.Key changes include:

      •  New targets for recycled materials in vehicles, particularly plastics

      •  Requirements for cars to be easier to dismantle, reuse and recycle

      •  Stronger controls on how end-of-life vehicles are processed and exported

This approach aims to reduce waste, protect critical raw materials and strengthen European supply chains. It’s a long-term strategy that supports sustainability without relying solely on electrification as the answer.

**A Softer Stance on the Petrol and Diesel Ban**

Alongside the circular economy deal, the EU appears to be rethinking its previous commitment to an outright ban on new petrol and diesel cars by 2035.

Rather than a hard stop, policymakers are now leaning towards emissions-reduction targets that allow some flexibility — potentially keeping hybrids, alternative fuels and limited internal combustion options in the mix for longer. Some reports even suggest a possible delay to 2040.

This shift reflects growing concern from manufacturers who argue that the original timeline was too rigid, particularly given rising costs, infrastructure gaps and uneven consumer readiness.

**Supporting Struggling European Manufacturers**

European carmakers have found themselves under intense pressure in recent years. The cost of electrification, combined with strict regulation and global supply chain challenges, has coincided with fierce competition from Chinese manufacturers producing well-equipped, competitively priced EVs at speed.

The EU’s more flexible stance could:

      •  Give manufacturers time to adapt production without sacrificing profitability

      •  Protect jobs tied to traditional powertrain manufacturing

      •  Encourage a broader mix of technologies rather than a single solution

However, the challenge remains: delay too much, and Europe risks falling behind in the global EV race. The balance between protection and progress will be critical.

**What This Means for the UK Car Market**

Although the UK is no longer part of the EU, European policy still has a direct impact on our market. Many vehicles sold in the UK are designed and built to EU standards, and manufacturers rarely develop separate strategies for each region.If the EU moves more slowly than the UK on petrol and diesel bans, we could see:

      •  Greater model availability for ICE and hybrid vehicles

      •  Potential pricing and supply inconsistencies

      •  Increased pressure on the UK government to reassess its own timelines

For consumers and fleets, this could mean more choice in the short to medium term — but also more uncertainty around long-term planning.

**The EV Debate: Still Divided** The conversation around EVs remains polarised. Supporters highlight their role in reducing emissions and improving air quality, while critics point to infrastructure challenges, affordability concerns and real-world suitability for certain drivers and businesses.

What’s becoming clearer is that a one-size-fits-all solution doesn’t work. Electrification is essential, but so is pragmatism. Hybrids, improved combustion engines and sustainable fuel alternatives all have a role to play during the transition.

**Looking Ahead**

The EU’s recent policy changes signal a more measured approach to automotive transformation — one that recognises economic pressures as well as environmental goals.

For leasing customers, this matters. Vehicle availability, residual values and total cost of ownership are all shaped by regulation. At Horizon Vehicle Leasing, our focus remains on helping customers choose the right vehicle for their needs today, while keeping one eye firmly on what tomorrow brings.

The road ahead isn’t straightforward — but with clearer thinking and balanced policy, it can still lead to a more sustainable and resilient automotive industry.

Why Miles per kWh Will Soon Matter More Than EV Range

**Miles per kWh vs MPG: Why EV Efficiency Will Soon Matter More Than Range**

Horizon Vehicle Leasing — Your expert guide to smarter electric vehicle choices

As electric vehicles become more popular across the UK, drivers are asking new questions about running costs, charging habits and day-to-day efficiency. But one question is more important than many people realise:

**Why are we all focused on EV range, instead of the EV equivalent of MPG — miles per kWh?**

When choosing a petrol or diesel car, motorists have always cared about MPG. We never asked “How many miles can I do on a full tank?” because filling up was quick, easy and familiar. But with EVs, range anxiety has taken centre stage — even though miles per kWh is the true measure of electric vehicle efficiency.

In this article, Horizon Vehicle Leasing explains why miles per kWh is becoming essential, how it impacts running costs, and when it will matter just as much as MPG.

**What Is Miles per kWh — and Why Is It More Important Than EV Range?**

Miles per kWh (m/kWh) tells you how efficiently an electric car uses its battery power.

It’s the EV equivalent of MPG and directly affects:

     •Your real-world electricity usage

     •Your home charging time

     •Your total running costs

     •How often you need to charge on long journeys

Two EVs may offer the same 250-mile range, but one might be far more efficient — using fewer kilowatt-hours to achieve that distance. As energy prices fluctuate, this efficiency difference matters more than ever.

Range tells you how far you can go once. Efficiency tells you how much it costs every time.

**Why Drivers Never Cared About ‘Miles per Tank’ — But Care About Range**

With fuel cars, nobody needed to know the distance per tank because:

     •Filling up took minutes

     •Fuel stations were everywhere

     •Tank size didn’t matter — MPG did

With EVs, charging takes longer and chargers vary in availability and speed. That shift made people focus on **total range**, not **efficiency**.

But this mindset is temporary.

As the charging network grows and rapid charging becomes quicker, **EV drivers will naturally start comparing miles per kWh just as they compare MPG today.**

**How Miles per kWh Affects Charging Costs and Energy Tariffs**

One of the biggest reasons miles per kWh will soon become essential is the rise of smart tariffs and off-peak charging.

Most off-peak energy windows last **4–5 hours overnight**.

But modern EV batteries (80–100 kWh or more) may take:

     • **8–10+ hours** to fully charge on a home charger

     •Longer still if the car is larger or less efficient

This means part of the battery charges at **higher daytime rates**, increasing overall cost.

**Efficient EVs need fewer kWh — meaning more savings at cheaper off-peak rates.**

Just as high-MPG cars saved money at the pump, high-efficiency EVs save money while you sleep.

**Why EV Efficiency Will Become a Key Leasing Decision**

For anyone leasing an electric car — whether privately or through a company — **miles per kWh affects long-term affordability.**

Two EVs with similar range might have wildly different running costs:

     •Streamlined hatchbacks often exceed **4+ mi/kWh**

     •Larger SUVs may average **2.5–3 mi/kWh**

At Horizon Vehicle Leasing, we’re already seeing leasing customers ask more questions about efficiency, real-world winter performance, and energy usage per mile.

As electricity prices remain unpredictable, **efficiency will become one of the most important factors in choosing the right EV lease**.

**When Will Miles per kWh Become as Important as MPG?**

We’re approaching a turning point.Miles per kWh will become mainstream when:

     •Rapid charging becomes more widely available

     •Average charging speeds continue to increase

     •Drivers become more familiar with EV ownership

     •Manufacturers highlight efficiency in brochures and advertising

     •Reviewers and comparison sites place efficiency at the top of the spec sheet

In other words: as EVs become normal, **mi/kWh becomes the new MPG.**

Range anxiety fades, and true efficiency takes over.

**The Future of EV Shopping: Efficiency First, Range Second**

Range will always matter to some extent, but it will stop being the headline figure. Instead, drivers will increasingly ask:

     •“How efficient is this EV?”

     •“How many miles will I get per kWh?”

     •“How much will this EV cost me to run each month?”

For leasing customers, these questions are critical — because the right EV isn’t just about how far it goes, **but how efficiently and affordably it gets there.**

**Conclusion: Miles per kWh Is the Next Big Metric in EV Buying**

Just as MPG shaped decades of petrol and diesel ownership, **miles per kWh will shape the next generation of electric motoring**.

As charging becomes faster and easier, range will matter less — and efficiency will matter more.

At Horizon Vehicle Leasing, we help drivers make informed EV decisions by comparing real-world efficiency, charging behaviour and long-term running costs. If you're considering an electric vehicle, understanding miles per kWh will help you choose a model that suits your lifestyle and your budget.

Electric Car Grant (ECG) scheme

The Government has announced a Grant of up to £3750 for electric vehicles with a list price of £37,000 or less. Here are some of the details.

There is £650 million available for the scheme.

The scheme will run until the 2028/29 financial year or until the funds run out.

To be eligible the cars must be electric with a list price of £37,000 or less.

The discounts offered are £1,500 and £3,750. £3,750 for vehicles that are produced to the highest sustainability standards, and £1,500 for cars that meet some but not all environmental criteria.

The scheme is available for orders placed from 16th July 2025.

Overview

Eligibility Criteria

To qualify the vehicles must be Class M1 passenger vehicles.

They must include a 3-year / 60,000 mile warranty (minimum).

They must offer an 8-year / 100,000 mile battery and drive train warranty.

The cars must maintain a battery capacity of: 80% for the first 3 years and 70% for 8 years or 100,000 miles.

Who Can Benefit?

The grant is available to:

Private Customers

Businesses

Salary Sacrifice Schemes

Company Car Providers

There is no cap on fleet orders, and the grant will be administered via a central portal, similar to the previous Plug-in Car Grant.
